back: A geographic area containing one or more datacenters, networked together with low latency. Some services (like Entra ID) are global, not region-specific.
Two regions in the same broad geography, linked for disaster recovery and staggered planned maintenance so both aren't updated at the same time.
A physically separate datacenter within a region, with independent power, cooling, and networking. Regions with zone support have a minimum of three.
A logical container for related resources, used for lifecycle management, access control, and billing organization. Deleting it deletes everything inside.
A billing and access-management boundary linked to an Azure account; also enforces quotas like max VM cores.
A container above subscriptions that lets policy and RBAC cascade down to many subscriptions at once. Can be nested up to 6 levels.
Management Groups → Subscriptions → Resource Groups → Resources.
Name/value labels for organizing resources (cost tracking, automation) independent of the resource group hierarchy. A resource can have several; tags don't control access.
IaaS compute — full control over the OS, customer manages patching. Billed by size, OS, storage, and networking.
Deploys and manages identical, load-balanced VMs that can automatically scale out/in based on demand.
PaaS for hosting web apps/APIs. Supports deployment slots to stage and swap a new version into production with minimal downtime.
ACI: simple, single-container, short-lived workloads. AKS: managed Kubernetes for orchestrating many containers at scale in production.
Serverless compute — small, event-triggered code (HTTP, timer, queue). Consumption plan bills only for actual execution.
A logically isolated network for Azure resources; can be segmented into subnets and peered with other VNets, even across regions.
Encrypted connection between a VNet and on-prem/another VNet, traveling over the public internet.
Private, dedicated connection to Azure that bypasses the public internet — more reliable and lower latency than VPN Gateway.
Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) traffic distribution — fast, protocol-agnostic, no HTTP awareness.
Layer 7 load balancer — routes based on HTTP attributes like URL path; can host a Web Application Firewall (WAF).
Global, DNS-based traffic router across regions — directs clients to the best-performing or nearest healthy endpoint.
Caches static content (images, video, scripts) at edge locations closer to users to reduce latency.
Basic, stateful firewall filtering traffic by source/destination/port/protocol, attachable to subnets or NICs.
Managed, centralized firewall with FQDN filtering and threat intelligence — goes beyond NSGs' basic IP/port filtering.
Secure RDP/SSH to a VM through the Azure Portal, without ever giving the VM a public IP address.
Blob: unstructured objects. Files: SMB/NFS shares. Queue: messaging. Table: NoSQL key-value. Disk: VM-attached storage.
Hot: frequent access, higher storage cost. Cool: infrequent (30+ days). Archive: rare (180+ days), lowest cost, hours to rehydrate.
Locally Redundant Storage — 3 copies within one datacenter. Protects against hardware failure only.
Zone-Redundant Storage — copies across 3 availability zones. Protects against a datacenter/zone outage.
Geo-Redundant Storage — LRS plus async copy to a paired region. RA-GRS additionally allows reading that secondary copy anytime.
Fully managed PaaS relational database (SQL Server engine) — Microsoft handles patching, backups, and HA.
Globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database with configurable consistency levels and single-digit-millisecond latency.
Cloud identity and access management service (formerly Azure AD) — global service, not tied to a region.
Multi-Factor Authentication — requires two or more verification methods, reducing risk from a stolen password alone.
Contributor: full access to manage resources, but can't grant access to others. Owner: full access plus can manage permissions.
Entra ID feature enforcing rule-based access policies (e.g. require MFA outside the corporate network).
